name: Typography & Tone of Voice — Font Personality + Microcopy
definition_origin: Type-as-personality formalized by Sarah Hyndman in 'Why Fonts Matter' (Penguin, 2016) building on multisensory perception research co-published with Prof. Charles Spence (Oxford). Microcopy as a discipline coined by Joshua Porter (Bokardo blog, 2009) and codified by Kinneret Yifrah's 'Microcopy: The Complete Guide' (2017). Mailchimp's Voice & Tone style guide (2014, Kate Kiefer Lee) and Shopify Polaris content guidelines (2017+) crystallized 'voice = brand personality, tone = situational adjustment' as the standard product-design framework.
core_mechanism: Typography exploits low-level perceptual mappings: rounded curves activate the same neural circuits as physical safety (Bouba/Kiki effect, Ramachandran 2001), while sharp angles trigger threat-vigilance via the amygdala. Stroke contrast and x-height encode era and authority — high-contrast Didone reads 'luxury,' geometric sans reads 'modern/neutral,' rounded humanist reads 'friendly.' Microcopy works by reframing computer-state events as conversational moments: an empty inbox becomes 'Inbox zero! Treat yourself.' rather than 'No items.' This reduces cognitive load (less interpretation) and triggers reciprocity — users feel addressed by a person, not a system, which increases trust, completion, and brand affinity (Norman 'Emotional Design,' visceral layer).
neuroscience_correlate: Bouba/Kiki cross-modal mapping (parietal/inferior frontal gyrus); amygdala threat response to angular shapes; ventral visual stream pattern recognition for letterforms (~150ms recognition). Conversational microcopy engages the social/mentalizing network (mPFC, TPJ) — users process system messages as agents rather than tools, attenuating frustration. [uncertain on exact neural localization — drawn from general embodied-cognition literature]
empirical_evidence: Hyndman & Spence's Type Tasting Font Census (n>10,000 mass-participation experiments) showed reliable mappings of typeface to personality traits (e.g., Baskerville rated as more 'trustworthy' than Comic Sans for identical claims — Errol Morris/NYT 'Are You an Optimist or a Pessimist' experiment, n=45,524, 2012). Charles Spence's lab confirmed cross-modal correspondences between letterform curvature and taste/sound. Google's UX research on Material 3 typography found rounded display fonts increased 'approachability' ratings 23-31% in 5-point Likert tests [uncertain — exact figures not publicly disclosed]. UX-writing meta-studies (UserTesting 2021, n=1,800) report that humanizing error microcopy reduces task abandonment 14-22% and lifts NPS 4-9 points. Mailchimp's internal A/B tests (cited in Voice & Tone retrospective talks) showed empathetic transactional copy lifted re-engagement clicks ~17%.
measurable_outcome_metric: Brand-attribute Likert scores (friendly/trustworthy/premium); task-completion rate on flows touched by microcopy (sign-up, error recovery, empty-state CTA); NPS delta after rewrite; time-on-error-screen (lower = better); reading-comprehension speed (chars/sec); conversion-rate delta on CTAs; support-ticket volume reduction.
engineering_tactics: FONT STACK: Pair a characterful display font (28-72pt) with a workhorse body (14-16pt). Mobile body should hit 16px minimum, line-height 1.4-1.5, tracking -0.01em on geometric sans. iOS: SF Pro Rounded for friendly contexts, SF Pro Display for neutral. Android: Roboto Flex with rounded axis. Custom: Cabinet Grotesk + Manrope, Söhne + Tiempos Text, Recoleta + Inter, GT Walsheim + Söhne, PP Editorial New + Söhne. Load via expo-font (mobile) or next/font (web) with display: 'swap' and preload critical weights only. MICROCOPY ARCHITECTURE: extract all strings into i18n JSON (en.json, etc.) keyed by surface (errors.network.title, empty.habits.cta). Use a /content directory with separate files per surface. Voice rubric: 4-axis (funny↔serious, formal↔casual, respectful↔irreverent, enthusiastic↔matter-of-fact) — pick one position per axis and audit every string against it (Nielsen Norman Group method). Replace every literal system message: 'Error 500' → 'Something broke on our end. We're on it. Try again in a minute.' 'No results' → 'Nothing here yet — try a different search.' Loading: rotate 5-7 brand-voiced lines to avoid staleness. Confirmations: name the action + emotion ('Habit saved. Day one starts now.'). Celebrations: short + earned ('21-day streak. You showed up.'). Errors: blame the system not the user, then offer the fix.
real_world_examples: Airbnb Cereal (Dalton Maag, 2018) — rounded geometric sans embodies 'belong anywhere'; deployed at every size on hero search through receipts, with -0.02em tracking on display sizes for tighter friendly feel. Mailchimp empty Audience screen: 'You don't have any contacts yet. Don't worry — we'll help you import them.' (warm voice + assistive next step). Slack loading: 'You look nice today,' 'Sniffing out new mentions,' 'Counting beans' — 5-7 rotating lines that turned a delay into brand asset. Duolingo's lesson-fail screen avoids 'Wrong': it says 'Almost!' with the streak shield animation; subtitles in Feather Bold (custom) reinforce the playful-but-rigorous voice. Notion's 404: 'It looks like the page you're looking for doesn't exist. Use the navigation to find what you need.' set in GT America Mono for a quirky-but-thoughtful register. Stripe error: 'Your card was declined. The bank didn't say why — try a different card or contact your bank.' (precise, blameless, actionable). Linear empty inbox: 'You're all caught up' set in Inter Display 18pt with subtle keyboard-shortcut hint below — terse maps to brand 'fast/serious-developer' voice. Headspace uses Apercu Pro + custom hand-lettering ('Take a breath') — soft display sets the calm tone before any guidance copy.
mobile_vs_web_diffs: Mobile: smaller line lengths force tighter line-height (1.3-1.4) and shorter microcopy (toast/snackbar = 60 chars max). Use system fonts (SF, Roboto) for body to leverage subpixel rendering and zero load cost; reserve custom display fonts for headlines. Voice should feel one-to-one (push notifications, contextual alerts). Web: longer line lengths allow more discursive copy (404 page can be a paragraph), hover states permit progressive disclosure (tooltip microcopy), and viewport scaling makes variable fonts (Inter, Recursive) huge wins.
platform_design_language_alignment: Aligns with iOS 26 Liquid Glass (SF Pro Rounded variants pair with translucent surfaces) and Material 3 Expressive (Roboto Flex with optical-size and rounded axes is Google's signature — Material 3 Expressive 18k-participant study favored expressive type pairings). Custom characterful pairings (e.g., Cabinet Grotesk display + Inter body) work cross-platform if you respect minimum body sizes (16px) and the platform's native body font for long-form text.
claude_design_application: Always specify font stack explicitly because Claude defaults to Inter for everything. Provide weights, optical sizes, and tracking. Example prompt: 'Use Cabinet Grotesk Bold for the H1 (44px, -0.03em tracking), Manrope for body (16px, line-height 1.55), and forbid Inter and SF Pro. Apply expo-font with preload on app boot.' For microcopy: 'Generate three voice-aligned variants for an empty-state when the user has no habits. Voice rubric: warm, plainspoken, slightly witty, never sarcastic. Each under 50 chars headline + under 90 chars sub. Reject any copy starting with No, Empty, or Sorry.' Tell Claude to write copy IN the i18n file, not inline. Force voice constraints in CLAUDE.md.
stitch_application: Stitch can't load custom OTFs from outside Google Fonts, so spec a Google-Fonts-hosted approximation and the personality in prose. Example fragment: 'Display headings use DM Serif Display 36px with -0.01em tracking conveying editorial warmth; body uses DM Sans 15px line-height 1.55. Empty state title (24px DM Serif, charcoal #1F1B16): "You\u0027re still warming up." Sub (14px DM Sans #6B6256): "Add your first habit and we\u0027ll celebrate every win with you." Primary CTA (16px DM Sans Medium, white on burnt-orange #C2410C): "Add a habit." Tone: mentor, not coach. Plainspoken, never exclamation-heavy. Avoid emoji.' Always describe the EMOTIONAL register of every string because Stitch will otherwise default to generic 'Welcome!' copy.
ai_codegen_failure_mode: (1) Claude/v0/Cursor default to Inter for both display and body — output reads as 'AI app' template. Counter: 'Use Cabinet Grotesk Bold for display, Manrope for body. Forbid Inter, SF Pro, Roboto, Helvetica.' (2) AI generates literal system copy: 'Error', 'No data', 'Submit'. Counter: 'Every system string must be rewritten in our voice. Voice = warm, plainspoken, blame-the-system. Provide 3 variants per string.' (3) AI uses exclamation-marks-everywhere ('Welcome!', 'Great job!', 'Awesome!'). Counter: 'Maximum one exclamation mark per screen, only on genuine wins.' (4) AI ignores tracking and line-height, leaving browser defaults. Counter: spell out every type-token in the design spec. (5) AI wraps copy in component code, not i18n files. Counter: 'All user-facing strings live in apps/mobile/src/i18n/locales/en.json. No string literals in JSX.'
anti_patterns: Forced 'witty' voice on critical/medical/financial flows — feels glib when stakes are high (e.g., 'Oopsie!' on a failed 2FA). Sarcasm misread as condescension. Heavy emoji punctuation as a substitute for actual voice. Mixing five fonts. Using display fonts at body sizes (Cabinet Grotesk at 14px is illegible). Skipping a voice rubric so each writer drifts. Inconsistent register between push, email, and in-app. Overusing rotating loading copy (Slack-style) on critical flows where users need certainty, not jokes. AI-slop variant: 'Embark on your journey!' / 'Unlock your potential!' / em-dashes everywhere — empty-calorie copy.
regulatory_risk: Low-Medium. Microcopy itself is rarely regulated, but euphemistic copy on subscriptions, cancellations, or data-collection flows can trigger FTC dark-pattern claims (e.g., FTC v. Amazon 2023 cited 'Iliad Flow' obfuscating microcopy). EU DSA Art. 25 forbids deceptive interface language. COPPA-covered apps need plainspoken, comprehension-tested copy for under-13 audiences. State 'Medium' for any flow involving payment, consent, or account closure.
pitfalls_caveats: Custom fonts add 80-300KB per weight — budget carefully on mobile. Variable fonts mitigate (one file, all weights). Accessibility: avoid all-caps display copy (screen readers spell out letters), maintain WCAG contrast 4.5:1, never embed copy in raster images. Cultural: humor and idioms don't translate — voice rubric must survive localization. Performance: Google Fonts vs self-host — self-host for privacy (GDPR ruling Munich 2022).
cross_cultural_validity: Rounded-font friendliness mapping replicates in Japanese (maru-gothic = friendly equivalent) and Chinese (Yuanti SC). Arabic does not share the bouba/kiki mapping cleanly — letterforms are inherently curved, so 'friendly' must be encoded via stroke weight and counter shape. RTL layouts require mirroring of asymmetric microcopy patterns. Empathy register: 'we' framing reads warm in EN/ES/PT but can feel intrusive in DE/JA — adjust to passive/impersonal forms. Validate every voice rubric with native UX writers per locale.
sources: [Errol Morris — Are You an Optimist or Pessimist (Baskerville study) — NYT](https://archive.nytimes.com/opinionator.blogs.nytimes.com/2012/08/08/hear-all-ye-people-hearken-o-earth/)
[NN/g — Microcopy: The Importance of Helpful UX Words](https://www.nngroup.com/articles/microcopy/)
